# Share, Bond & Debenture Valuation (PRICE & YIELD Functions)

## Learning Outcomes

- Analyse automobile company share data
- Apply financial formulas (Gordon Growth Model, P/E Ratio, Dividend Yield)
- Create charts for valuation comparison
- Perform sorting and filtering for investment analysis

***

## Dataset 

<div style="overflow-x:auto;">

| Company | EPS (Rs) | DIV % | Share Price (Rs) | Face Value (Rs) | Annual Dividend (Rs) | Dividend Yield % | P/E Ratio | Intrinsic Value* | Over/Under Valued |
|--------|------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Bajaj | 11.9 | 0.50 | 275.0 | 10 | 5.0 | 1.82% | 23.11 | 71.43 | Overvalued |
| Hero Honda | 10.2 | 0.22 | 135.0 | 10 | 2.2 | 1.63% | 13.24 | 31.43 | Overvalued |
| Kinetic | 12.0 | 0.25 | 177.5 | 10 | 2.5 | 1.41% | 14.79 | 35.71 | Overvalued |
| M & M | 20.1 | 0.25 | 205.0 | 10 | 2.5 | 1.22% | 10.20 | 35.71 | Overvalued |

*\* Intrinsic Value assumes Required Return (K)=12%, Growth Rate (g)=5%*

</div>

*📁 Download Excel File*  
[Download Financial Data Excel](Valuation.xlsx)

***

## Data Entry & Formulas

### Structure in Excel

| Column | Description |
|--------|------------|
| A | Company |
| B–E | Inputs (EPS, DIV%, Price, Face Value) |
| F | Annual Dividend |
| G | Dividend Yield % |
| H | P/E Ratio |
| I | Intrinsic Value* |
| J | Over/Under Valued |

### Formulas Used (Assuming data starts in Row 3)

| Calculation | Formula |
|------------|--------|
| Annual Dividend | `=E3*C3` *(Face Value × DIV %)* |
| Dividend Yield % | `=F3/D3` *(Annual Dividend / Share Price)* |
| P/E Ratio | `=D3/B3` *(Share Price / EPS)* |
| Intrinsic Value | `=F3/(0.12-0.05)` *(Annual Div / (K - g))* |
| Over/Under Valued | `=IF(D3>I3, "Overvalued", "Undervalued")` |

### Averages Row (Bottom Row)

| Calculation | Formula |
|------------|--------|
| Average EPS | `=AVERAGE(B3:B6)` |
| Average P/E Ratio | `=AVERAGE(H3:H6)` |
| Average Yield | `=AVERAGE(G3:G6)` |

***

## Chart Creation

### Recommended Charts
- **Clustered Column Chart** &rarr; Compare Share Price vs. Intrinsic Value
- **Bar Chart** &rarr; P/E Ratio comparison across companies

### Diagram 

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
A[Select Data] --> B[Insert Tab]
B --> C[Choose Chart]
```

### Insight
- All analyzed automobile shares are currently trading above their calculated intrinsic value.
- M & M offers the lowest P/E ratio, while Bajaj has the highest dividend yield.

***

## Chart Formatting

- **Title:** *Share Price vs. Intrinsic Value*
- **X-axis:** Company
- **Y-axis:** Value (Rs)
- **Legend:** Share Price, Intrinsic Value

***

## Interpretation

The analysis reveals that the shares across all four automobile companies (Bajaj, Hero Honda, Kinetic, and M & M) are currently overvalued based on the Gordon Growth Model (assuming a 12% required return and 5% growth rate). Despite being overvalued, Bajaj offers the best dividend yield at **1.82%**, while M & M appears relatively cheaper compared to earnings with the lowest P/E ratio of **10.20**.

***

## Sorting

### Example
- **Sort by P/E Ratio (Smallest to Largest)** &rarr; Identify the cheapest stock relative to earnings.
- **Sort by Dividend Yield % (Largest to Smallest)** &rarr; Identify the best income-generating stock.

***

## Advanced Filter

### Example Criteria

**Criteria 1:**
| Over/Under Valued |
|--------|
| Undervalued |

**OR Criteria 2:**
| P/E Ratio |
|----------|
| < 15 |

***

## Final Output
- Multi-company financial Excel analysis
- Charts showing valuation comparisons
- Summary interpretation of stock pricing

***

## Conclusion
This experiment demonstrates how Excel tools and financial formulas can be used effectively for stock valuation, comparative analysis, and investment decision-making.
